data.title: Elastic Q3 Earnings: Why Investors Are Buying Into The AI Growth Story
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Elastic (NYSE:ESTC) delivered a strong fiscal Q3 2025 earnings report that saw the stock jump 15% after hours.

What's particularly interesting to note is that the market right now is just looking for a reason to take profits off the table, as the risk-off mood is very prevalent.

With that in mind, it shows that investors are still seeking well-positioned growth stories and are happy to back companies that can show how they are benefitting from AI, and improving their underlying profitability.

Accordingly, Elastic, with a 5% net cash to market cap position, is an attractive investment at 41x forward free cash flow.

Rapid Recap

Back in November, I made a bullish call on ESTC where I stated,

I now believe, that in this brave new market, paying 46x next year's free cash flow for ESTC is a good entry point.

And it's interesting to note that since that day, roughly 90 days ago, the stock hasn't participated in much of the upside or the downside of the market.

Elastic's Near-Term Prospects

Elastic helps businesses search. That's the core of its offering.

Customers use Elastic to analyze their own data to deliver insights. This can be data logs or documents, rigid or fluid data.

Elastic's main product, Elasticsearch, makes it easy for companies to find insights.

On top of that, Elastic is also a big player in observability, which helps businesses monitor and troubleshoot their systems, even though that space is becoming very crowded.

Also, Elastic competes in cybersecurity which is even more crowded!

But essentially, the key to its offering is that they consolidate many different IT requirements onto one platform, platformization, which saves customers money while improving efficiency.

Indeed, from their earnings call, we heard the same refrain that they've been making for a while, that AI is a big growth driver and that customers continue to look for ways to consolidate their tech stack.

Given this background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates of 16% Y/Y in Fiscal 2026

Not only did Elastic raise its full-year fiscal 2026 outlook yet again, but it also beat analysts' fiscal Q3 2025 revenues by more than 3 percentage points.

This is terrific and gives investors a lot of confidence that the business still has some fuel left in the tank and sets up its prospects for fiscal year 2026 to grow by at least 16% y/y, if not a little faster.

On top of that, keep in mind that fiscal Q4 2025 is not only up against the most challenging comparable of the prior year, but also, there are fewer days in fiscal Q4 2025 than in any of the first three quarters of fiscal 2025.

All in all, I'm reasonably confident that Elastic can grow its topline by around 16% y/y, which makes Elastic a rather interesting growth opportunity.

With that in mind, let's now discuss its valuation.

ESTC Stock Valuation -- 41x Forward Free Cash Flow

Elastic holds a net cash position of approximately 5% of its market cap, if we include the after-hours pop in its share price. This means that Elastic has a strong balance sheet that is supportive of the bull case.

Now, before we go further ahead, consider this quote from the earnings call,

[...] we expect to expand non-GAAP operating margin only modestly in fiscal '26 relative to our current non-GAAP operating margin guidance for fiscal '25. We expect these investments to be more front-end loaded in fiscal '26 as our sales kick off and engineering Global All Hands will both be in Q1 of FY '26. We will provide more detail on our Q4 earnings call.

Elastic's non-GAAP operating margins may reach 16% at some point in the next twelve months.

Even if not exactly in fiscal year 2026, it will be on a path toward this profit margin at some point in the next twelve months, even as we consider the fact that Elastic will be front-loading investment in its operations.

Therefore, we can see a path for Elastic's non-GAAP operating profits to reach about $280 million.

Now, keep in mind that Elastic likes to guide towards its adjusted free cash flow figure. However, this metric removes interest payments, as if interest payments is a non-existent pesky detraction.

However, from a clean free cash flow figure estimate, I believe that Elastic's free cash flow margin could reach around 17% in fiscal 2026.

Altogether, I believe that Elastic is able to get to about $300 million of clean free each flow at some point in the coming twelve months.

This leaves the stock priced at 41x forward free cash flow. A figure that is acceptable, for investors that want exposure to Elastic's growth engine.

Risk Factors to Consider

Back in summer 2024, Elastic had some sales execution issues, which caused investors to lose confidence in management's strategy. This has left this business in a penalty box and is now to some extent, a ''show-me'' story before investors get too excited about Elastic.

In other words, I believe it may take more than just 2 good quarters before investors fully buy back into Elastic's narrative.

On top of that, Elastic competes against the likes of Datadog (DDOG) and Splunk (which has now been acquired by Cisco (CSCO)) in the observability space and for analyzing logs.

Even though Elastic has a strong market share due to its open-source roots, none of its peers are pushovers either.

The Bottom Line

Elastic is proving that it belongs in the conversation when it comes to high-growth, AI-driven software companies. With a solid 16%+ revenue growth outlook, improving margins, and a strong balance sheet, this stock has the fundamentals to back up its momentum.

Sure, it faces tough competition, but its ability to consolidate IT needs into one platform gives it a real advantage.

At 41x forward free cash flow, it's not a steal, but for those looking to bet on continued growth and efficiency in enterprise search, observability, and security, Elastic looks like a compelling investment.

The Fund's exposure to dividend-paying common stocks and high-quality, short-duration bonds contributed to its outperformance versus the benchmark during the year. Notable performers included Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL), Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) and Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (CCEP), which increased by 115%, 24%, and 18%, respectively. Performance detractors include the common stocks of United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) and Genuine Parts Company (GPC), which declined by -15% and -13%, respectively. The Fund's longer-duration bond holdings saw low single-digit declines as interest rates increased.

In a twist of fate, interest rates on longer-dated bonds have increased as the Federal Reserve reduced short-term rates by 1% during the latter half of the year. A stronger-than-expected economy, significant fiscal deficits by the Federal Government and lingering inflation present challenges to further lower short-term interest rates this year. Corporate bond spreads have continued to narrow near historic lows. Investors should exercise caution when adding credit exposure at this time. This is particularly important for low credit quality bonds, where investors are not being adequately compensated for the additional credit risk. The Bond Fund will continue to be managed in a conservative manner by keeping bond maturities in the short-to-intermediate range and the credit quality high. Additionally, high-quality, dividend-paying common stocks continue to offer an attractive combination of income and price appreciation potential.

IMPORTANT INFORMATION FOR INVESTORS

1 11/9/73 is the inception date for the oldest class of shares, Class A shares. Class I shares were first offered on 9/4/01; returns prior to this date are those of Class A shares and have not been adjusted for expenses; otherwise, returns would vary.

2 It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section on the last page of this fact sheet.

3“Net (what you pay)” represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

4 Listed holdings reflect the largest portions of the fund’s total and may change at any time. They are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Data is expressed as a percentage of net assets and excludes cash and cash equivalents.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit j hinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

Most segments of the U.S. bond market sold off in the quarter, dampening total returns for the calendar year. Economic growth expectations improved, due in part to the outcome of the November U.S. elections. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) made two quarter-point interest-rate cuts, adding to the half-point reduction it enacted in September. Bonds nevertheless came under pressure from concerns about stubborn inflation, as well as Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s hawkish comments on the likely path of monetary policy in 2025.

U.S. Treasuries were adversely affected by these developments, with longer-term bonds experiencing the weakest results. The yield on the 10-year note (US10Y) surged from 3.81% at the end of September to 4.58% on December 31. Mortgage-backed securities, which tend to have elevated sensitivity to the direction of the Treasury market, sold off in response. Asset-backed securities (ABS) and commercial mortgage-backed securities outpaced MBS at a time in which investors displayed a preference for higher-yielding, credit-oriented categories.

Investment-grade corporates also suffered losses even though yield spreads continued to decline toward multi-year lows. High-yield bonds registered a narrow gain and outperformed investment-grade debt as the improving economic outlook provided firm support for investors’ appetite for risk. The category further benefited from its lower degree of interest-rate sensitivity.

Contributors and detractors

The fund’s yield curve positioning, primarily its overweight in the 5-to 10-year portion of the curve, hurt its fourth-quarter relative performance. The resulting shortfall was offset, to some extent, by a contribution from asset allocation. The fund benefited from its underweight in U.S. Treasuries and overweights in high-yield bonds, investment-grade corporates, and ABS. An overweight in agency MBS detracted. Security selection contributed to results, with the majority of the positive impact occurring in the investment-grade corporate space.

Portfolio changes

We maintained a stable portfolio positioning in the final three months of the year, with minor shifts that were driven largely by bottom-up selection. We added slightly to high yield and ABS, while making a modest reduction in U.S. Treasuries. We kept a significant overweight in agency MBS on the view that the category continued to offer compelling relative value and a source of higher quality. Duration (interest-rate sensitivity) remained neutral versus the benchmark, and the fund was overweight in intermediate-term bonds in anticipation of additional steepening in the yield curve.

In corporates, we maintained an overweight in financials on the belief that the sector was attractive on a relative basis. We also continued to focus on the non-cyclical and utilities sectors, while moving up in quality and focusing on shorter and intermediate maturities. The corporate yield curve was relatively flat, meaning that there was little benefit to taking on added risk by moving further out on the maturity spectrum. In addition, we believed an emphasis on higher-quality, shorter-dated debt could provide a measure of ballast if market conditions became less favorable. With corporate yield spreads at the tight end of the historic range, we maintained the view that income and security selection would be paramount. Although we didn’t look to increase the portfolio’s overall risk profile, we sought opportunities to add incremental yield through individual securities offering attractive relative value.

For the year ended December 31, 2024, performance was driven primarily by significant contributions from the Information Technology, Industrial, and Financial sectors. The Information Technology sector's performance was propelled by the semiconductor giant Nvidia Corporation, which returned an astounding 171% during the year, with additional support from Ciena Corporation's (CIEN) impressive 79% gain. The Industrial holdings saw strength across the board with performance scattered around the sector's 16% total return. Financials had a total return of 21%, its smaller relative weighting limited its overall impact on the Fund's total performance.

The largest relative detractors of performance for the year were Health Care, Real Estate and Communication Services. The Health Care sector was down -14%, driven by weak performance from Iqvia Holdings, Inc. (IQV) and Chemed Corporation (CHE). Real Estate had a single holding, SBA Communications Corporation (SBAC), which was down -18% over the year. The Communications Services sector, represented by a single holding, also underperformed the broader market, delivering a return of -3%.

The Fund's goal is to purchase shares of exceptional companies at attractive prices with the expectations of earning favorable returns over the long run.

data.title: Henkel: Tariff Risks Probably Somewhat Limited, Has European Discount
data.description:
Henkel AG (OTCPK:HENKY) (OTCPK:HENOY) (OTCPK:HELKF) delivered a solid quarter in the previous results, led primarily by Europe. The more profitable adhesives segment suffered a bit in the US. While the company expects much of the positives to continue into Q4 particularly in terms of volume, driven by the recovery in electronics demand, somewhat offset by the pressures in mobility. Hair continues to stand out as a positive performer, and there has also been a solid contribution from a growing MRO area within adhesives. We also want to mention that while Henkel is a globalised company, other than the macroeconomic effects that tariffs could have on key markets, it appears at least that at their scale they have quite a lot of manufacturing in each of the markets that they serve, including the US in hair, with Loctite adhesives and across other products as well. In terms of valuation, we want to comment that as usual and as reiterated in our previous coverage, the preferred and common shares behave similarly to each other and the multiples from the screener reflect the combined market cap of those shares. Taking those valuations, we think Henkel actually looks reasonably priced when compared to a possible US peer in 3M (MMM). However, with minor risks from tariffs haranguing European and APAC macro, and also questions around holiday season demand for electronics, we aren't rushing to buy this as the price of the stock has progressed normally and in line with the solid operational developments.

Latest Earnings

At a group level, adhesive technologies saw both solid volumes and decent pricing, with volumes carrying the organic growth. The industrial slowdown in the US meant that this segment struggled a bit there, while it performed strongly in Europe and other geographies, the former supported by emerging growth areas like MRO for aerospace markets, with almost no Boeing (BA) exposure as a matter of fact, and the latter geographies supported by the recovery in electronics, like in APAC, where a lot of the adhesives for automotive and electronics are based. Pricing pressure also came from formula-based pricing, which is more common with packaging solutions and with industrial customers - general commodity deflation meant automatically lower pricing. While EV demand is solid in China and provided respite for mobility revenues, grouped together in the more segmented reporting by Henkel, other markets saw slowdowns in automotive consistent with higher rates and demand destruction from inflation in most other geographies. Craftsman and construction revenues was supported by emerging geographies.

Price drove the consumer brands business, where they have been focusing efforts on optimising their portfolio. Europe saw some pressure in laundry and home care in terms of volumes. In North America, consumers were also under pressure in addition to adhesives, with higher promotional intensity and geographies experiencing trading down, contributing to negative volume effects compared to cheaper peers that would exist in their markets. Across geographies, including LatAm and APAC, hair products are performing very well through brands like Schwarzkopf and Joico, contributing to some of the premiumisation effects we're seeing in consumers at a group level. There is double-digit growth in styling products, and these markets are addressed with local manufacturing, Joico being a primary brand for the US.

Bottom Line

The company expects the volume trends to continue into the end of the year. Some slight source of concern was comments by one analyst that Covestro was beginning to comment that electronics demand into the holiday season, to be reported in the Q4, wasn't looking as great. Other outlets reported similarly mixed signals in terms of electronics demand. This may hamper Q4 results, as much has lately been based on that long-overdue electronics recovery.

We don't think tariff risks are particularly severe, at least directly, for Henkel. They have important facilities in the US supporting their hair markets, production of adhesives for automotive including Loctite brand, and other consumer products in home care and laundry. From what we were able to ascertain, they have duplicated manufacturing footprints for their various markets to address each geography. This plays in their favour amid tariff risks on EU companies. We do have a macroeconomic concern which is that the large ex-US markets may suffer if tariffs are placed on them by the world's biggest consumer, and this may hamper the recovery in adhesive technologies that drives the company margins, with the US having given demand slack across products this quarter.

However, in terms of valuation, we like Henkel. While they are more expensive than Arkema (OTCPK:ARKAF) (5.5x), which has brands like Bostik, versus a pretty relevant comp in 3M, Henkel looks attractive with an 8.3x EV/EBITDA multiple against the 13x at 3M. They share similar features such as adhesive exposures backed by brands, large industrial and consumer markets, electronics exposures and similar financial profiles in terms of sales volatility and double-digit EBIT margin businesses. We'd rather take the plunge with Henkel, particularly as we don't think there are especially greater direct tariff risks at Henkel compared to 3M. But the price has progressed solidly in line with operational progress, and there are minor risks to the demand picture for the business as mentioned. We aren't racing to buy it at the moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Global Diversification In Our Model Portfolios
data.description:
By Andrew Okrongly, CFA & Joe Tenaglia, CFA, CMT

In investing, it's easy to let recency bias dictate our decisions. The past decade-plus of U.S. equity dominance has been remarkable, leading many investors to question the need for international diversification. But history tells us that markets move in cycles.

Why We Still Believe in International Diversification

While our Model Portfolios maintain an over-weight allocation to U.S. equities, we continue to see strategic value in international diversification.

  • Market Leadership Is Cyclical—The U.S. has dominated international equities since the global financial crisis (GFC), but this is just one chapter in a longer story. In prior decades, international stocks have held extended periods of outperformance, often when the U.S. faced headwinds. Betting that this cycle will continue indefinitely is a risky assumption.
  • Diversification Benefits—Different regional equity markets do not move in tandem. Less-than-perfect correlations between international and U.S. stocks mean that adding global exposure can reduce portfolio risk, providing a buffer when U.S. markets hit turbulence.
  • U.S. Equity Concentration Risk—The S&P 500 is now overwhelmingly weighted toward a handful of mega-cap technology firms. While these companies have driven returns, concentration risk is increasing. Diversifying internationally means accessing sectors and geographies that may offer different growth drivers.
  • Corporate Domicile Is Increasingly Arbitrary—The concept of a company's "home country" is blurring. Many U.S.-listed companies derive a significant portion of their revenues from international markets, while many non-U.S. companies are deeply embedded in the global economy. Looking beyond the U.S. gives investors exposure to the full breadth of global growth.

Positioning within International Equities

Within our international equity allocations, two countries stand out—Japan in developed markets and India in emerging markets.

Japan: A Compelling Valuation Story

Japan is often overlooked by investors, but we see it as an attractive opportunity:

  • Earnings Yield Advantage—Japanese equities offer strong earnings yields relative to global peers, creating a valuation cushion.
  • Corporate Reform Momentum—Japan's corporate governance reforms, including capital efficiency measures and focusing on shareholder returns, are creating a more attractive investment environment.
  • Geopolitical Tailwinds—In an era of shifting supply chains, Japan is a key player benefiting from geopolitical realignments.

Within our Japan exposure, we prefer to have a tilt toward export-oriented companies. Exporters are a key component of the WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ), which offers investors access to Japan's global market presence while mitigating currency risk.

India: A Structural Growth Powerhouse

If Japan is the value play, India is the long-term growth engine:

  • Demographics and Workforce Expansion—With a young and expanding working-age population, India is primed for sustained economic expansion.
  • Massive Infrastructure Investments—The government's commitment to roads, rail and digital infrastructure is laying the foundation for decades of growth.
  • Financial Sector Growth and Digital Revolution—Increased access to credit, digital banking advances and the rise of fintech are accelerating economic activity.
  • Corporate-Friendly Reforms—Recent tax cuts and incentives for manufacturing and technology make India an increasingly attractive destination for capital.

A potential obstacle to an over-weight allocation to India is its elevated valuations compared to other emerging markets. The WisdomTree India Earnings Fund (EPI) addresses this concern through its earnings-weighted methodology, inherently incorporating valuation sensitivity into its strategy. This approach often results in lower valuation metrics compared to market cap-weighted approaches. Notably, EPI's estimated price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is currently below that of the S&P 500 Index.

The Case for Currency Hedging in Developed International Markets

When U.S. investors allocate internationally, they often do so without hedging against currency risk, leaving their portfolios vulnerable to fluctuations in foreign exchange rates. This unhedged approach effectively places a bet against the U.S. dollar's strength. We advocate for hedging at least 50% of currency risk in our developed international equity portfolios. Here's why:

  • Reducing Uncompensated Risk—Currency fluctuations can add unnecessary volatility to a portfolio without offering a long-term return premium.
  • U.S. Dollar as a Hedge against Market Stress—Historically, the U.S. dollar has been negatively correlated with equity market downturns, making it an effective hedge in times of global volatility.
  • Carry Trade Advantage—With U.S. interest rates higher than those in Europe and Japan, currency hedging can actually add to returns due to the positive interest rate differential.

Final Thoughts

It's tempting to believe that U.S. equity dominance will last forever. But history suggests otherwise. International allocations provide portfolio diversification, access to different growth stories and a hedge against concentrated U.S. market risks.

While we maintain an over-weight allocation to U.S. equities, our Model Portfolios continue to embrace international diversification—not because it's the consensus view, but because long-term investors should prepare for what comes next, not just what's worked in the past.

Important Risks Related to this Article

WisdomTree Model Portfolio information is designed to be used by financial advisors solely as an educational resource, along with other potential resources advisors may consider, in providing services to their end clients. WisdomTree’s Model Portfolios and related content are for information only and are not intended to provide, and should not be relied on for, tax, legal, accounting, investment or financial planning advice by WisdomTree, nor should any WisdomTree Model Portfolio information be considered or relied upon as investment advice or as a recommendation from WisdomTree, including regarding the use or suitability of any WisdomTree Model Portfolio, any particular security or any particular strategy.

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty

DXJ: The Fund focuses its investments in Japan, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Japan that can adversely affect performance. Investments in currency involve additional special risks, such as credit risk and interest rate fluctuations. Derivative investments can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities, and more sensitive to the effect of varied economic conditions. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers. Due to the investment strategy of this Fund it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time.

EPI: This Fund focuses its investments in India, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments associated with the region, which can adversely affect performance. Investments in emerging, offshore or frontier markets such as India are generally less liquid and less efficient than investments in developed markets and are subject to additional risks, such as risks of adverse governmental regulation and intervention or political developments. As this Fund has a high concentration in some sectors, the Fund can be adversely affected by changes in those sectors. Due to the investment strategy of this Fund it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs.

Andrew Okrongly, CFA, Director, Model Portfolios

Andrew Okrongly joined WisdomTree in 2022 as a Director on the Model Portfolios Team. He is responsible for the design and ongoing management of model portfolios and custom solutions for portfolio managers and advisors. Andrew is also a member of the Model Portfolio Investment Committee. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Andrew was a Director on the Outsourced Chief Investment Officer (OCIO) team at Commonfund, where he was responsible for macro-economic analysis and advising institutional clients on strategic and tactical asset allocation. Andrew began his career at BlackRock where he held a variety of fixed income and multi-asset investment roles. Andrew received a BBA degree from the University of Michigan and is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst designation.

Joe Tenaglia, CFA, CMT, Director, Model Portfolios

Joe Tenaglia joined WisdomTree as Asset Allocation Strategist in 2016. He is responsible for building and managing WisdomTree’s CIO-managed and custom model portfolios. In this role, he focuses on asset allocation, security selection, and portfolio construction for the firm’s open architecture models, with objectives ranging from strategic to tactical. Joe sits on WisdomTree’s Model Portfolio Investment Committee, which oversees the firm’s ETF models and helps shape and communicate WisdomTree’s thoughts on the markets. Prior to joining WisdomTree, he held roles at Emerging Global Advisors (acquired by Columbia Threadneedle) and BNY Mellon. Joe received his B.S. in Finance and Marketing from Boston College. He is a holder of both the Chartered Financial Analyst designation and Chartered Market Technician designation.

Original Post

data.title: United Airlines: Now Is The Right Time To Buy The Stock
data.description:
Investment Thesis

United Airlines Holdings (NASDAQ:UAL) is an attractive investment asset because it combines a strong competitive position, diversified sources of income, and a long-term growth strategy. The company remains undervalued compared to its key competitors.

I am assigning the company a Buy rating, with a target price of $117 per share with a 21% growth potential in 2025. In the coming years, the growth drivers will be the expansion of international routes, fleet upgrades and an increase in the share of premium services, which should have a positive impact on profitability.

Introduction

United Airlines Holdings, based in Chicago, is one of the largest airlines in the world, having formed its current large-scale network after merging with Continental Airlines in 2010. This period was accompanied by integration problems and inefficiency, but in 2015, under the leadership of Oscar Munoz, the company focused on improving its corporate culture and customer service. By 2020, UAL had significantly expanded its network of long-haul flights and upgraded its hub model, but the COVID-19 pandemic led to massive cost reductions and temporary fleet conservation. The recovery began in 2022, and in 2023 the company returned to pre-crisis levels, setting records for passenger traffic in the summer season.

United's revenue continues to be based on passenger transportation, which accounts for about 90% of the company's operating revenue. This includes both domestic flights to the United States and extensive international routes spanning six continents (Asia, Europe, Africa, Latin America, the Middle East and the Pacific). In 2024, against the backdrop of an active market recovery, domestic destinations and the transatlantic turned out to be the strongest, but the Asian segment, which had long suffered from closed borders, also began to catch up dynamically. At the same time, United's clientele is very diverse: from individual tourists looking for great deals to corporate business passengers who are willing to pay for premium service and priorities. In addition to its core transportation activities, UAL receives revenue from United Cargo shipments and loyalty programs—this is especially noticeable within MileagePlus, one of the largest in the industry, where mile bonuses and co-branded credit cards generate impressive additional revenue.

Geographically, United's route network is large, especially among North American carriers: more than 300 destinations under its own flag and hundreds more through codeshare partners and participation in the global Star Alliance. The company has eight main hubs distributed across the United States: Chicago (ORD), Denver, Houston (IAH), Los Angeles (LAX), Newark, San Francisco (SFO), Washington (IAD) and Guam. This hub model makes it possible to effectively combine local (origin/destination) and transfer flows, offering a high frequency of flights.

After the 2010 merger and the bankruptcy period in the 2000s, United Airlines focused on cost reduction, but now the company's priority is quality of service and profitability growth. As part of the United Next strategy, launched in 2021, UAL is implementing a large-scale fleet upgrade:the company plans to purchase more than 700 new aircraft by 2033, replacing outdated models with spacious Airbus A321neo and Boeing 737-10. This will reduce the unit cost of transportation, increase the number of premium seats and increase profitability. In the long-haul segment, United is betting on expanding the Boeing 787 fleet, which will strengthen its position on transatlantic and trans-Pacific routes. At the same time, the premium segment is developing: Polaris in business class and Premium Plus (improved economy) have been introduced, and Basic Economy budget tariffs have been retained to compete with low-cost airlines. The MileagePlus loyalty program has become an important source of profit, and partnership with Chase Bank in the field of issuing credit cards brings in hundreds of millions of dollars annually. The implementation of the strategy looks reasonable: by the beginning of 2024, the company had accumulated about $20 billion in liquidity, reserved slots for the supply of aircraft and signed long-term contracts with trade unions, ensuring relative stability.

Competitors

By 2024, the US commercial passenger aviation market has almost returned to pre-crisis levels.

According to the IATA forecast, the aviation industry in North America will maintain a moderate growth rate in the coming years, demonstrating a CAGR of 2.2% until 2040. This confirms the key role of the United States as the largest aviation market, accounting for about 23% of global passenger kilometers (RPK). In the long term, global passenger traffic is expected to double by 2040, with an average annual growth rate of 3.4%. The total number of passengers will increase from 4 billion in 2019 to over 8 billion by the end of the forecast period. The Asia Pacific region will make the largest contribution to this growth, due to favorable demographic factors and rising incomes, contributing to increased demand for air transportation.

The North American market is dominated by the four largest airlines [American Airlines (AAL), Delta Air Lines (DAL), United, and Southwest Airlines (LUV)], controlling over 65% of domestic traffic. UAL has an approximately 16% share, which puts it in fourth place in terms of domestic flights, while American, Delta and Southwest each have 17-18%. At the same time, smaller low-cost carriers [JetBlue (JBLU), Alaska Air Group (ALK), Spirit Airlines (OTC:SAVEQ), Frontier Group Holdings (ULCC)] and regional airlines are competing for the rest of the market. The competition covers several segments: domestic flights, international destinations (especially transatlantic and Asia), and the difference between premium and budget service. United is actively developing long-haul routes and premium service classes, while low-cost airlines are focused on minimum fares.

Over the past three years, the industry has gone through a difficult recovery path after COVID-19. At the height of the pandemic, the demand for flights almost collapsed, but by 2022, domestic traffic reached 85% of the level of 2019, and in 2023, long-haul destinations began to grow rapidly. One of the drivers is "revenge travel": pent-up demand has pushed people to travel, leading to record seat occupancy rates reaching 83.5% in 2024. Economic factors also contributed: according to the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, U.S. GDP grew by about 2.5% in 2023, while employment remained high, which supported leisure and business purchasing power. In addition, the opening of borders with Europe and Asia has increased the demand for international flights, and many travelers have moved to the premium segment, preferring business class or improved economy.

However, the risks of stagnation remain. In the event of a recession or escalation of geopolitical tensions, the demand for travel may drop sharply (especially in the corporate segment). The increase in fuel prices also directly affects the costs of airlines and their desire to shift costs to passengers. In addition, business tourism has not returned to full scale: according to the Global Business Travel Association, the volume of business trips remains at 70–80% of the pre-pandemic level. There is also a risk of excess capacity if airlines begin to aggressively expand their fleet in pursuit of market share, and possible consolidation (for example, the potential merger of JetBlue and Spirit) can redistribute the players' positions.

The structure of the American aviation market as a whole is close to an oligopoly. Only four major carriers retain most of the passenger traffic, and high entry barriers (cost of fleets, certifications, airport slots) prevent the emergence of new major competitors. At the same time, each airline is trying to distinguish itself by its service and route network, which gives the market the features of monopolistic competition. For United, this configuration has advantages: it is easier to avoid harsh price wars, and the position in the "big four" provides visible market power. The downside is that any major action (for example, ticket sales by a competitor) forces UAL to react by ceding some of the margin, and the antimonopoly authorities can intervene if conditions for passengers worsen.

Financials

In the 2024 reporting year, the company earned $57.063 billion, which is 6.23% YoY. The growth was driven by a record number of passengers transported (174 million people, +145 new mainline flights per day), increased premium (+10%) and corporate transportation (+7%), as well as increased revenue from the loyalty program (+12%) and freight transportation (+30%).

I expect revenue to grow by 15.33% in 2025 to about $65.81 billion due to a further increase in passenger traffic, especially on international routes, where demand continues to recover. The CAGR of total revenue between 2020 and 2024 was 38.4%. This growth was largely due to the low base of 2020 and the rapid recovery of the industry. According to my forecast, the CAGR will slow down to 13.92% from 2025–2030, as the industry enters a stable growth phase after a period of rapid recovery.

The company's EBITDA amounted to $8.13 billion in 2024, showing a drop of 4.4% YoY. The average annual EBITDA growth rate from 2022 to 2024 is 29.43%. The EBITDA margin for the reporting period was 14.26%, remaining at the level of 2023. The company's interest payments on debt have a big impact on EBITDA margin, which is why I don't expect profitability to grow in 2025. Additionally, increased operating and capital expenditures will put pressure on margins. The main pressure factors are rising personnel costs, fuel, depreciation of the new fleet, airport fees and technological investments.

Net profit for the year increased by 20% YoY and amounted to $3.15 billion. The current profit has become a record for the company. The net profit margin was 5.52%, and the ROE was 28.63%.

By the end of 2024, the company is demonstrating a reduction in its debt burden, which is confirmed by an improvement in key ratios. Debt to Equity decreased to 4.84x (compared to 6.63x in 2023), which indicates a decrease in the share of borrowed funds in the capital. Net Debt/EBITDA also improved, falling to 2.35x compared to 2.87x in 2023, indicating a strengthening in the company's ability to repay net debt from operating income. The Interest Coverage Ratio has increased to 3.97x, indicating improved interest payment service capabilities. However, the current Liquidity Ratio has decreased to 0.81x, which may indicate a lower level of current assets compared to short-term liabilities. In general, the company demonstrates a steady reduction in its debt burden, but maintaining low liquidity requires monitoring short-term liabilities.

In 2024, the company's capital expenditures decreased by 22%, falling from $7.1 billion to $5.6 billion due to temporary cost optimization for fleet and infrastructure upgrades, as well as a more restrained company strategy in an unstable macroeconomic environment. I expect Capex to grow by 32% in 2025, due to the accelerated implementation of the fleet renewal program, orders for new aircraft, investments in technological improvements and modernization of airport hubs.

In 2025, I expect free cash flow (FCF) to decrease to $1.9 billion, due to increased capital expenditures under the fleet renewal program, as well as increased operating costs amid high inflation in personnel, fuel, and infrastructure costs. In the long term, I predict that the free cash flow CAGR in 2025–2030 will be about 19.23%, due to several factors. Firstly, the completion of major capital projects by 2027 will reduce the pressure on investment costs, which will lead to an increase in operating cash flow. Secondly, a large-scale modernization of the fleet and an increase in fuel efficiency will lead to lower operating costs, improving business margins. Thirdly, the growth of passenger traffic, an increase in the share of premium services and the development of loyalty programs will ensure a more stable cash flow. In addition, the expected slowdown in cost growth after 2026 will also be a factor in FCF recovery, which will allow the company to improve cash generation in 2027–2030.

Thus, the company's financial position remains stable: moderate debt burden and high operational efficiency make it possible to maintain stability even in the face of rising capital costs. However, in 2025, a temporary decrease in free cash flow is expected due to active investments in fleet and infrastructure upgrades, which in the long term should lead to increased profitability and improved financial performance.

Valuation

I believe that the company's shares, despite the 2.6-fold price increase since August, still retain the potential for growth in 2025, although growth will be slower than in 2024. Despite the strongest growth, the multipliers are close to their historical values, and the P/E and EV/EBITDA discounts have decreased to almost zero to their median values.

At the same time, compared to its competitors, the company remains undervalued: in terms of P/E, the underestimation is about 15% compared to the median among competitors, and in terms of EV/EBITDA, the company is undervalued by 14% compared to competitors. For comparison, I took key competitors or companies relatively similar to it: Delta Air Lines, American Airlines, Southwest Airlines, Ryanair (RYAAY) and others.

My thesis is that the company is actually close to its fair value when compared with both historical values and competitors. For comparison, I took the key competitors, or companies relatively similar to it: PACCAR (PCAR), Cummins (CMI), Komatsu (OTCPK:KMTUY) (OTCPK:KMTUF), Deere & Company (DE) and others.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 50%), target value of P/E and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights (estimated weights of 25%). In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 2% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 6.73%.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 10.35x, and EV/EBITDA is 6.32x (I took the median historical averages).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of United shares is $117 per share, with a potential for growth of 21% in 2025.

Risks and growth drivers

It is difficult to imagine a more dynamic industry than aviation, and therefore even a major player like United Airlines has to balance on the edge of several threats. First of all, the global economy: if a recession starts in the United States or abroad, business travel and expensive international routes will be the first to suffer, and it is the premium segment and long-haul flights that UAL is betting on. Any escalation of geopolitical tensions (sanctions, closure of airspace, instability in destination countries) can also dramatically undermine plans to expand the network. In terms of price, fuel remains the most vulnerable factor: kerosene costs can "eat up" an impressive portion of profits because operational efficiency is not unlimited yet—and despite the depreciation of old aircraft, the United fleet requires enormous maintenance and upgrade costs. Another weak point is operational reliability, which is inferior to Delta by some metrics: delays or disruptions in hubs like Chicago and San Francisco lead to chain cancellations, and in the eyes of passengers it harms reputation. In addition, high salaries and regular negotiations with trade unions (when there is a shortage of pilots and mechanics in the market) can lead to strikes or a significant increase in personnel costs. Don't forget about the competition: low-cost airlines (Spirit, Frontier) continue to attract some of the price-sensitive audience, while Delta and American compete closely for business customers and valuable slots at key airports. Let's add regulators who are ready to intervene in case of monopolistic manifestations or a significant increase in tariffs. As a result, any coordinated action by competitors, an unfavorable macro environment, or disruption of plans to upgrade the fleet can affect financial results and give a negative signal to the stock exchange, dropping United's quotes. The situation is aggravated by plans to modernize the fleet: despite the obvious benefits, a significant increase in capital expenditures can burden cash flows, and delays in the delivery of new aircraft force older aircraft to operate longer with higher repair and fuel costs, negatively affecting profitability.

On the other hand, United has a powerful set of levers in its hands to accelerate growth and strengthen its position in both the domestic and international markets. First of all, it is a global route network and participation in the Star Alliance: UAL is able to rapidly increase its presence by opening new flights and codeshare agreements, especially when transatlantic and trans-Pacific destinations show historically high utilization. Investments in the United Next project, which provides for the purchase of more than 700 aircraft by 2033, allow the company to upgrade its fleet: new liners are more economical, more spacious and more attractive for premium travelers. The MileagePlus loyalty program plays an essential role: it not only retains loyal customers, but also generates stable cash flow from partnerships with banks (credit cards) and major retailers. In addition, the market situation in the United States is still developing in favor of the "big four": with limited opportunities for new strong players to enter, UAL can safely increase its operating margin by improving its service and keeping tariffs at a competitive but profitable level. The increased demand for premium class is also a plus—people are willing to pay for convenience after the pandemic, and United has already introduced Polaris and Premium Plus, which bring higher profitability due to expensive tickets and additional services. Finally, the growing popularity of the "bleisure" format (a mix of leisure and business) encourages the filling of expensive seats, so with reasonable operational discipline, the company has every chance to maximize profits from this trend and attract new loyal passengers, which will directly affect the growth of quotations.

Conclusion

United Airlines remains one of the leading global airlines with a strong route network, a sustainable business model, and a growth strategy focused on fleet modernization and service improvement. Despite challenges in the form of rising personnel, fuel and capital investment costs, the company is successfully recovering its financial performance after the pandemic, demonstrating steady revenue and profit growth. United Airlines has the potential for long-term growth, supported by global demand for air travel, loyalty programs, and strategic partnerships. Based on current estimates, I believe that the company's shares remain undervalued and recommend buying them.
